title: Executive Order 11126—Establishing a Committee and a Council Relating to the Status of Women

flags.justification: A broadly representative structure: a seven-member Cabinet-level interdepartmental Committee plus a seventeen-member Citizens' Advisory Council, both coordination-focused rather than concentrating operational authority.
Sec. 103 requires an annual consolidated report to the President covering both bodies' activities.
Two related bodies with explicit cross-information-sharing (Sec. 102(5)) coherently serve one purpose.
Coordination and advisory functions with no binding compliance regime against third parties.
No compliance regime exists for an incentive structure to attach to.
A standing coordination body for an ongoing policy concern, evergreen by nature, consistent with EO 9775.
This creates new coordination and advisory structures rather than removing any existing oversight.
The seventeen-member Citizens' Advisory Council provides genuine, independent, non-governmental external input by design.

The direct institutional follow-through on the President's Commission on the Status of Women's landmark 1963 report, establishing lasting Cabinet-level coordination machinery that fed directly into subsequent employment-discrimination and civil-rights legislation.
Broad, flexible membership with provision for temporary participation by other relevant agency heads (Sec. 101(b)), a well-designed, non-conflicting structure.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (negative). Structural weight: 1/12 = 8.3%. Issued November 1, 1963, three weeks before President Kennedy's assassination — among his last substantive domestic-policy orders. Genuinely historically significant as the direct institutional follow-up to the Commission on the Status of Women (chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt), whose report helped lay groundwork for the Equal Pay Act and later Title VII protections, yet scores low due to its careful, coordination-focused, broadly representative design.
